title: Linear Algebra
description: Vectors, matrices, and transforms — the language of neural networks.

questions.prompt: If $\mathbf{u} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}$, then $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} =$
questions.explanation: Add componentwise: $\begin{pmatrix} 1+3 \\ 2+4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix}$.

questions.prompt: The dot product $\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} =$
questions.explanation:
$$\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2 = 2 \cdot 4 + 3 \cdot 1 = 8 + 3 = 11.$$

The result is a scalar.

questions.prompt: Two nonzero vectors are orthogonal if and only if their dot product is:
questions.explanation:
By definition, $\mathbf{u} \perp \mathbf{v}$ iff $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$.

This is widely used in projections and PCA.

questions.prompt: The Euclidean norm $\left\|\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\right\| =$
questions.explanation:
$$\|\mathbf{v}\| = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5.$$

This is the familiar 3–4–5 triangle.

questions.prompt: If $A$ is $2 \times 3$ and $B$ is $3 \times 4$, then $AB$ has shape:
questions.explanation:
Matrix product $AB$ is defined when the inner dimensions match ($3 = 3$).

The result has shape $(\text{rows of } A) \times (\text{cols of } B) = 2 \times 4$.

questions.prompt: Compute $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.explanation:
Row–column products: first entry $1\cdot 3 + 2\cdot 4 = 11$.

Second entry $0\cdot 3 + 1\cdot 4 = 4$.

So the result is $\begin{pmatrix} 11 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}$.

questions.prompt: The identity matrix $I$ satisfies $AI = A$ when shapes match. For $2 \times 2$, $I =$
questions.explanation:
The identity has $1$s on the diagonal and $0$s elsewhere.

It acts like $1$ for matrix multiplication.

questions.prompt: $\det\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix} =$
questions.explanation:
For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$, $\det = ad - bc$.

Here $2\cdot 4 - 1\cdot 3 = 8 - 3 = 5$.

questions.prompt: A square matrix $A$ is invertible if and only if:
questions.explanation:
$A$ is invertible iff $\det(A) \neq 0$ (equivalently, full rank / nontrivial nullspace is empty).

If $\det(A)=0$, columns are linearly dependent.

questions.prompt: The transpose of $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$ is shape:
questions.explanation:
Transpose swaps rows and columns: a $2 \times 3$ matrix becomes $3 \times 2$.

Entry $(i,j)$ of $A$ becomes entry $(j,i)$ of $A^T$.

questions.prompt: If $A$ is invertible, $(AB)^{-1} =$ (when $B$ is also invertible):
questions.explanation:
The inverse of a product reverses the order: $(AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}$.

Check: $(AB)(B^{-1}A^{-1}) = A(BB^{-1})A^{-1} = I$.

questions.prompt: Rank of a matrix is:
choices: Dimension of its column space (max independent columns)
questions.explanation:
Rank is the dimension of the column space (equivalently row space).

It counts how many linearly independent columns (or rows) the matrix has — crucial for understanding feature matrices in ML.

questions.prompt: Eigenvectors of $A$ satisfy $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ with $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$. Here $\lambda$ is called:
questions.explanation:
$\lambda$ is an eigenvalue; $\mathbf{v}$ is a corresponding eigenvector.

Eigen-decompositions appear in PCA, spectral methods, and stability analysis.

questions.prompt: For $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$, an eigenvalue is:
questions.explanation:
A diagonal matrix has eigenvalues equal to its diagonal entries: $2$ and $3$.

For example $A\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = 2\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$.

questions.prompt: The trace of $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ is:
questions.explanation:
Trace is the sum of diagonal entries: $1 + 4 = 5$.

Also $\mathrm{tr}(A) = \sum_i \lambda_i$ (eigenvalues counted with multiplicity).

questions.prompt: A matrix $Q$ is orthogonal if:
questions.explanation:
Orthogonal matrices satisfy $Q^T Q = I$ (columns are orthonormal), so $Q^{-1} = Q^T$.

They preserve lengths and angles — rotations/reflections.

questions.prompt: In $\mathbb{R}^n$, a set of vectors is linearly dependent if:
choices: One is a linear combination of the others (nontrivial relation $\sum c_i v_i = 0$)
There are fewer than $n$ of them
questions.explanation:
Linear dependence means a nontrivial linear combination equals zero — at least one vector is redundant.

Independence is required for bases and full-rank models.

questions.prompt: The matrix–vector product $A\mathbf{x}$ can be viewed as:
choices: A linear combination of the columns of $A$ with weights from $\mathbf{x}$
questions.explanation:
If $A = [\mathbf{a}_1 \,|\, \cdots \,|\, \mathbf{a}_n]$ and $\mathbf{x}=(x_1,\ldots,x_n)$, then $$A\mathbf{x} = x_1\mathbf{a}_1 + \cdots + x_n\mathbf{a}_n.$$

This is the column picture of neural network linear layers.

questions.prompt: Softmax is often applied to a vector $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^k$. The outputs:
choices: Are nonnegative and sum to $1$ (a probability distribution)
questions.explanation:
Softmax: $\mathrm{softmax}(z)_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$.

Each component is positive and the vector sums to $1$, so it can be read as class probabilities.

questions.prompt: For least squares, minimizing $\|A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}\|^2$ leads (when $A^T A$ is invertible) to:
questions.explanation:
Normal equations: $A^T A \mathbf{x} = A^T \mathbf{b}$.

If $A^T A$ is invertible, $\mathbf{x} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$ — the linear regression solution.
